streamreader.next() expects new lines at the end of each NMEASentence.

Try using read() instead of readline().

Example with python 2.7
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import pynmea2
import StringIO
lines="$GPGGA,183800.0,3203.121,N,03446.075,E,1,03,4.90,00038,M,017,M,,*5A\n $GPVTG,000.0,T,357.3,M,000.00,N,000.00,K*4C\n"

buf = StringIO.StringIO(lines)

print "Passing Lines from readlines()"
streamreader = pynmea2.NMEAStreamReader()
for line in buf.readlines():
    if line[0:6]=='$GPGGA':
            msg = pynmea2.parse(line.strip())
            print('msg1:'+str(msg))
    for msg in streamreader.next(line.strip()):
            print('msg:'+str(msg))

buf = StringIO.StringIO(lines)
print "Passing Lines from read()"
streamreader = pynmea2.NMEAStreamReader()
for msg in streamreader.next(buf.read()):
        print('msg:'+str(msg))
```

Yields
```
 Passing Lines from readlines()
 msg1:$GPGGA,183800.0,3203.121,N,03446.075,E,1,03,4.90,00038,M,017,M,,*5A
 Passing Lines from read()
 msg:$GPGGA,183800.0,3203.121,N,03446.075,E,1,03,4.90,00038,M,017,M,,*5A
 msg:$GPVTG,000.0,T,357.3,M,000.00,N,000.00,K*4C
```

This can be intentional and mean that the corner coordinates of the bounding box are outside of the image area after augmentation. Try `bboxs_aug.cut_out_of_image()` to cut off all parts of the BBs that are outside of the image, then you shouldn't get negative coordinates any more. You can also use `bboxs_aug.remove_out_of_image()` to completely remove BBs which are partly or fully outside of the image area and `bboxs_aug[i].is_partly_within_image()` to verify that the `i`th image is not fully outside of the image area. (Note that these functions are not thoroughly tested yet.)

At the time, I needed it, I created a custom function to format units which also does some more pretty printing. In case you're interested, this is the part that only formats the unit part:

```python
def get_raw_label(quantity):
    """Get the name of the unit, without enclosing brackets."""
    short = pint.unit.UnitsContainer(
        {ureg._get_symbol(key): value
         for key, value in quantity.units.items()})
    as_ratio = any(exp > 0 for _, exp in short.items())
    return pint.formatting.formatter(
        short.items(),
        as_ratio=as_ratio,
        single_denominator=True,
        product_fmt=u'·',
        division_fmt=u'/',
        power_fmt=u'{0}{1}',
        parentheses_fmt=u'({0})',
        exp_call=pint.formatting._pretty_fmt_exponent,
    )
```

Woops, sorry, here's a real patch.  I didn't really have time to look at in_cksum_add() so maybe that is what needs to be patched, but it makes sense to me to just use in_cksum() for TCP as it is used for IP.

--- ip.py	2010-03-25 22:53:51.000000000 -0400
+++ ip.py	2010-11-09 15:28:35.000000000 -0500
@@ -41,9 +41,8 @@
                 p = str(self.data)
                 s = dpkt.struct.pack('>4s4sxBH', self.src, self.dst,
                                      self.p, len(p))
-                s = dpkt.in_cksum_add(0, s)
-                s = dpkt.in_cksum_add(s, p)
-                self.data.sum = dpkt.in_cksum_done(s)
+                #  Get the checksum of concatenated pseudoheader+TCP packet
+                self.data.sum = dpkt.in_cksum(s+p)
                 if self.p == 17 and self.data.sum == 0:
                     self.data.sum = 0xffff	# RFC 768
                 \# XXX - skip transports which don't need the pseudoheader

I have just double checked, and the implementation makes sense as it is now.

To clarify the signs:
- The current in a load in the interface is positive for load effect and negative for generation effect. This means that if I create a load of 10kA it should make the voltage drop, and if I enter a load of -10kA it should make the voltage raise.

- Now under the hood, both power (`S`) and current (`I`) are summed into the `Sbus` and `Ibus` arrays with negative sign, hence my 10kA load would be `-10kA / (Vnominal * sqrt(3))` at the `Ibus` vector

- The `Ibus` vector (with presumably negative values for loads in it) is passed to the power flow algorithms.
  Now in the Jacobian, where that formula belongs:  `Ibus = Ybus * V - I `. The Ibus from the Jacobian formula is expected to be positive, that is why I add the currents injection vector with negative sign.

I hope It is clear. Nevertheless, if yu think there's an error, please let me know to fix it. My reasoning is that at the moment, the results make sense for the used signs.

First off, I totally agree that the current design of the TinyDB internals is quite messy. To my defense: the current architecture of `Table`s, `Element`s and `StorageProxy`s grew organically over time. And even though it's far from elegant I think so far they served their purpose well enough.

Now regarding a possible refactoring:

> If the goal is to maintain the eid attribute, then there seems to 3 ways to do so;
> [...]
> Append it to the object retrospectively, as is currently the case. At the very least, this could be improved so that instead of creating every Element everytime that StorageProxy.read() is hit, perhaps the table dict could have its `__getitem__` method overriden to do an isinstance() check?

As long as we don't introduce some kind of caching we have to create `Element` objects for every item every time the database is read from the storage. Another option would be shifting the responsibility for creating the `Element` objects to the storage which (1) would be a breaking change and (2) would require "smart" storages opposed to the current design where storages are rather dumb (more on that later).

> Have it as a field of the element. To solve the problem of conflicts, it could stored as something like `__eid__` in the elements dict?

As far as I can remember I considered this but chose to not follow this path as it basically introduces redundant information because we already store tables as `{id: {element ...}, id: {element ...}}`. Also, this too would be a breaking change as the way to access the element id would be an item access, not an attribute access.

> In fact, the way I see it currently, Element, StorageProxy and Table are all specific to the JSONStorage class, and are in fact a hindrance to implementing other storages.

I don't see how Element, StorageProxy and Table are specific to the JSONStorage. If they are, that's not what I intended. Could you elaborate in which way(s) these classes depend on implementation details of JSONStorage?

> My idea is that instead of the database/table objects telling the storage when to read and write, they should tell the storage what to do (as in get this, update this, delete this - basically a REST interface). In practice, this would basically mean integrating the tinyrecord package into the core (as it achieves basically the same goals).

As far as I can see we have to choose between two competing designs here: smart storages (your proposal) and dumb storages (the status quo). Now I'm sure there are good arguments for both approaches. The reason I like the dumb storages way is that implementing a new storage is very easy. You just have to be able to store/load a dict and you can do that in whatever way you like. In the end I don't think one design is inherently superior to the other but I think that the trade-offs taken with the dumb storages are best in terms of usability.

> To be brutally honest, if you really look at what TinyDB currently does, it pretty much loads a json file for me, and writes to it on occasion. I could do that myself with a few lines of code :/ I've seen in other issues that you don't want to integrate too much into core, but I think to get a truly flexible system, it needs certain minimum capabilites.

When I wrote the initial version of TinyDB the big innovation if you will was the query interface. In the end TinyDB is just that: a dict with a handy query interface. With that in mind I'm not sure whether or not TinyDB is the best base for handling graph structures.

A quick remark on breaking changes: Some of your proposals would require breaking changes which would affect a big share of users. As the popularity of TinyDB grows (>1000 GitHub stars) I'm becoming more and more conservative in that regard. That's not to say that breaking changes are ruled out but I feel like they should bring improvements to the majority of the userbase to be considered worth the upgrade.

---

Now back to the original question of customizing the `Element` class: I still believe the most straight-orward with the least amount of breakage for the users is introducing a class attribute for the TinyDB or Table instance like ELEMENT_CLASS. It's certainly not the best solution with regard to performance but performance is quite explicitly *not* a design goal of TinyDB.
As I think about it, I'd say I've designed TinyDB to have a nice API, not to be fast :smile:

I can see how that would be useful. One way to do it with the existing `pydeps` would be to create a Python source structure corresponding to your target language. E.g. for C you'd have to write a preprocessor that creates `foo/bar/baz.py` from `foo/bar/baz.c` and converts `#include` to `import`.  It should be doable, but...

`pydeps` uses a patched version of Python's `modulefinder.py` module which works by reading the bytecode of the "root" file looking for import op-codes (and then recursively reading each imported file). This is very Python specific, but an attempt has been made to generate more abstract datastructures after the initial pass.

It could be possible to create a better defined interface between the raw dependency finder and the rest of the system, but it turns out that is just half the problem. For any non-trivial program, the raw dependency graph is *huge*. You're rarely interested in the Python stdlib dependency graph when looking at your own module yet it turns out to be difficult to avoid it - iow, you'll need a language-sensitive dependency graph pruner.

It turns out checking `/proc/pid/status` is faster than `pid in pids()`. Patch:

```
diff --git a/psutil/_pslinux.py b/psutil/_pslinux.py
index 243f162..5be89d8 100644
--- a/psutil/_pslinux.py
+++ b/psutil/_pslinux.py
@@ -422,7 +422,19 @@ def pids():
 
 def pid_exists(pid):
     """Check For the existence of a unix pid."""
-    return _psposix.pid_exists(pid)
+    exists = _psposix.pid_exists(pid)
+    if exists:
+        try:
+            with open_binary("%s/%s/status" % (get_procfs_path(), pid)) as f:
+                for line in f:
+                    if line.startswith(b"Tgid:"):
+                        tgid = int(line.split()[1])
+                        return tgid == pid
+                raise ValueError("'Tgid' line not found")
+        except (EnvironmentError, ValueError):
+            return pid in pids()
+    else:
+        return exists
 
 
 # --- network
```

Let me brief on anyone who wants to make changes to accommodate the latest Bot API (4.0). I start with more trivial ones, then move on to harder ones.

--------

Telegram says:
> Added the field *thumb* to the Audio object to contain the thumbnail of the album cover to which the 
    music file belongs.

Key phrase is "Added the field *thumb* to the Audio object". In telepot, the habit is to represent API objects using Python dictionaries, so a new field in an object should not affect us. However, to facilitate accessing a field using `.` notation, there is a way to convert a dictionary into a namedtuple. As a result, every API object has a corresponding namedtuple. When a field is added to an object, we need to make a corresponding change to that namedtuple. The file is `namedtuple.py`. The original definition of Audio is:

```python
# incoming
Audio = _create_class('Audio', [
            'file_id',
            'duration',
            'performer',
            'title',
            'mime_type',
            'file_size'
        ])
```

With the addition of a *thumb* field of the type *PhotoSize*, it should be modified as:

```python
# incoming
Audio = _create_class('Audio', [
            'file_id',
            'duration',
            'performer',
            'title',
            'mime_type',
            'file_size',
            _Field('thumb', constructor=PhotoSize),
        ])
```

The *thumb* field looks more complicated than others because its type is *not* primitive (int, float, string, etc) and we need to tell it to interpret the dictionary in that place into another namedtuple. The "constructor" in this case is just the name of the target namedtuple, and can be considered a parsing hint.

Also note the comment above the namedtuple definition. They can be:

- incoming: meaning we only receive the object from Telegram servers, but never send it out
- outgoing: meaning we only send it out, but never receive it
- or both (rarely)

This distinction is important because:

- for incoming namedtuples, we must make sure all non-primitive fields be given a "constructor" like above. Otherwise, that field would remain a dictionary, which defeats the purpose of using namedtuples. This is not needed for *outgoing* namedtuples because fields are supplied by user, so no parsing hint is required,

- for outgoing namedtuples, some fields have *default* values. For example, `InlineQueryResultArticle`'s `type` field is default to `article`, as required by Bot API. In contrast, no field of any *incoming* namedtuples has default values.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added the field *animation* to the Message object. For backward compatibility, when this field is set, the document field will be also set.

Key phrase: Added the field animation to the Message object. Points to consider:

1. Message is an *incoming* namedtuple
2. the field *animation* is of the type Animation

Changes should be similar. I leave that to the reader as an exercise.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added support for Foursquare venues: added the new field *foursquare_type* to the objects Venue, InlineQueryResultVenue and InputVenueMessageContent, and the parameter *foursquare_type* to the sendVenue method.

Points to consider:

1. The field *foursquare_type* is of the type String. No parsing hint (if incoming) is needed.
2. Venue is incoming
3. InlineQueryResultVenue and InputVenueMessageContent are outgoing

I also leave the changes as an exercise.

As for the method *sendVenue*, I will delay the discussion until later, lumping it together with other *method* changes.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added vCard support when sharing contacts: added the field *vcard* to the objects Contact, InlineQueryResultContact, InputContactMessageContent and the method sendContact.

1. The field *vcard* is of the type String.
2. Contact is incoming
3. InlineQueryResultContact and InputContactMessageContent are outgoing

I will delay the discussion of the method *sendContact* similarly.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added support for editing the media content of messages: added the method editMessageMedia and new types InputMediaAnimation, InputMediaAudio, and InputMediaDocument.

Finally, we have to create *new* types of namedtuple here.

1. InputMediaAnimation, InputMediaAudio, and InputMediaDocument are all outgoing

Luckily, there are two InputMedia* siblings in existence already: InputMediaPhoto and InputMediaVideo. Find them, and use them as templates for the new members.

I will delay the discussion of the method *editMessageMedia* similarly.

--------

That's it for today. Good luck.

I'm not sure how it would've selected the correct one if the year isn't in the filename.
The refiner picks the first one that matches on the year.

Year is matched two times. First against the firstAired. If The video has no year this test is skipped. A second time agaist the seriesName itself split in parts (series + year) and here however matching is done in a stricter fashion so videos without year do not match series with TVDB names containing one (aliases included).

So `Once Upon A Time` will match `Once Upon A Time` but not `Once Upon A Time 2011` nor `Once Upon A Time (2011)`

> So I'd say that if there are external binary artifacts that are needed to setup GSSAPI integration fixtures then they should be downloaded in build_integration.sh . But the deployment of fixtures should probably be handled in the test.* modules as much as possible.

A have a few thoughts/questions about this:

**About the Kerberos build**

The Kerberos "fixture" differs a bit from Kafka in that it requires to be built for the local architecture. I was leaning towards having `build_integration.sh` use `apt`/`yum` to install the Kerberos service and then configure the service correctly for use with the integration tests. I believe this would be better since the available OS packages are already well integrated with the specific OS and would avoid having to install development packages altogether (gcc, make, bison, etc...)

The alternative would be to download the Kerberos source from MIT and build it as required, but I think this could be an overkill. Assuming that the integration tests will always run on RedHat or Debian OS's, having Kerberos installed through packages should suffice.

**Kerberos instances and configuration**

I would like to avoid having multiple Kerberos fixtures running at once on a single server using different ports and configurations. This is very unusual and could lead to unforeseen issues. It also seems unnecessary to me to start/stop the Kerberos KDC server before and after every tests.

I believe it's better to have a single Kerberos realm set up and used for all the integration tests. The approach that I had in mind was to complete the Kerberos configuration and startup in the 
`build_integration.sh` script and then pass the Kerberos realm and keytab as parameters to the integration tests through environment variables, in a similar way it's done with `KAFKA_VERSION`.

The Kerberos server and keytab would then be configured and started only once. When the tests are launched, if the `KERBEROS_REALM` and `KAFKA_KEYTAB` variables are set, all the tests will run with Kerberos integration and use GSSAPI authentication. If they aren't set, the same tests will run, with without SASL/GSSAPI.

A Python fixture could still be used to start/stop Kerberos, but I'm not sure how much value that would add. I'd be inclined to let the OS manage the service but if you think it's better to have a fixture to spawn/stop it I can look into it. In that case, I wonder it if would be better to build it from source to ensure installation prefix is controlled and not OS-dependent.

> @asdaraujo Given that we use pytest, you might find https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/fixture.html very convenient. It's beautiful magic in that it reduces a lot of boilerplate code in an optimized fashion (for example, see fixture scopes) while still being pretty clear what's actually happening behind the scenes.

I've decided to break down my PR in two: some changes to tests to pave way for the GSSAPI tests, and then the GSSAPI changes (this PR). I'm currently working on the improvements to the integration tests.

However, since most of the integration tests were using TestCase classes, I kept that convention and extended from there.

I'm still a bit unclear on some things.
For example, let's say we keep the default
```python
format = "CPU: {cpu_usage:.2f}%, Mem: {mem_used:.2f}/{mem_total:.2f} GB ({mem_used_percent:.2f}%)"
```
This should be split in two composites:
`"CPU: {cpu_usage:.2f}%"` and `"Mem: {mem_used:.2f}/{mem_total:.2f} GB ({mem_used_percent:.2f}%)"`. What about the `, ` in between? Which color should it be?
I don't think we can know without the user telling us where to split. Which means that the user should instead give:
```python
format= ["CPU: {cpu_usage:.2f}%", "Mem: {mem_used:.2f}/{mem_total:.2f} GB ({mem_used_percent:.2f}%)"]
```
This really changes the expectation for format. Additionally, the current behaviour is completely different: The *WHOLE* format changes color depending on the *MAX* between cpu and mem.

Unless I'm missing something obvious, we have two choices:
1. We change the expectations for format and use composites. I think this is more elegant but I don't see a way to replicate the "max between mem and cpu" part.
2. We do something like I did and allow for a list of values to check to change the color. This used to be useful because we couldn't color part of the output independently. I think it is obsolete now that we can.

What do you think?

@evanunderscore I had a quick look by dumping out the contents of sys.argv at https://github.com/openstack/git-upstream/blob/master/git_upstream/main.py#L126 and unfortunately although the program might be called on the CLI as `git -C .../ upstream` it's still invoked via `git-upstream` so there's no real way to determine from that approach.

But you did give me an idea, at the point that this is important, some of the `compgen|complete` related variables will have been set and I tested for their presence to determine whether to do this subparser idea.

```
    parent = parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog='git-upstream',
        description=__doc__.strip(),
        epilog='See "%(prog)s help COMMAND" for help on a specific command.',
        add_help=False)

    if 'COMP_LINE' in os.environ:
        import re
        if re.match("git .* upstream ", os.environ['COMP_LINE']):
            parser = parser.add_subparsers(
            ).add_parser('upstream', add_help=False)
```

Unfortunately this just seems to result in `upstream` being appended on each completion because it appears that to be some confusion on determining the next level of subcommand:

Running `COMP_LINE="git -C ../fixtures-git/ upstream " git -C ../fixtures-git upstream` outputs:
```
usage: git-upstream {upstream} ...

Command-line tool for tracking upstream revisions

positional arguments:
  {upstream}

See "git-upstream help COMMAND" for help on a specific command.
```

While subsequently running `COMP_LINE="git -C ../fixtures-git/ upstream " git -C ../fixtures-git upstream upstream` gets me the original help:
```
usage: git-upstream upstream [--version] [-h] [-q | -v] <command> ...

optional arguments:
  --version      show program's version number and exit
  -h, --help     show this help message and exit
  -q, --quiet    Suppress additional output except for errors, conflicts with
                 --verbose
  -v, --verbose  Increase verbosity from commands, conflicts with --quiet. May
                 be set more than once.

commands:
  <command>
    import       Import code from specified upstream branch.
    supersede    Mark a commit as superseded by a set of change-ids.
    help         Display help about this program or one of its commands.
    drop         Mark a commit as dropped.
```

I've experimented with injecting `git` into `sys.argv` ahead of the current command line just to see if it helps it understand to move onto the next level of subcommands in this case and unfortunately no joy.

Thanks for reporting. The concept is solid, but my fear is that this change enables completion to continue running in situations where the resulting string will raise an error in actual parsing, because not all positionals have been satisfied.

In other words, argcomplete's first order of business is to try as hard as possible to stick to argparse's default behavior, including indicating that parsing will fail by refusing to supply any more completions when mashing `<tab>`.

If you can think of a way to show that behavior is kept intact - for example, only skip the assertion if currently completing a positional (??? I need to think about this more) - then I'll be fine with this change.

(Cosmetic nitpick: "disabled" should probably be used instead of "removed")

代码修改如下：

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
#coding=utf-8

import os
import sys
import time 
import itchat

if os.name == 'posix' and sys.version_info[0] < 3:
	import subprocess32 as subprocess
else:
	import subprocess


help = '''\
WeChat remote:
* download file: aria2c url
* download video: youtube-dl url
* online video: mpv url
* & most commands you use in terminal
* help(show this message)\
'''

@itchat.msg_register('Text')
def interact_filehelper(msg):
	if msg['ToUserName'] != 'filehelper': return
		
	if msg['Text'] in ['help', u'帮助']:
		# print msg['Text']
		itchat.send(help, 'filehelper')
	else:
		commands = msg.get('Content', '')
		args = commands.split()
		try: 
			proc = subprocess.Popen(args,stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
			itchat.send(proc.communicate()[0].strip(), 'filehelper')
		except OSError as e:
			itchat.send('Commands/Files not found', 'filehelper')

itchat.auto_login(hotReload = True)
itchat.run()

```

In the readme there is sample code to overlay an image to a video:

```python
import ffmpeg

in_file = ffmpeg.input('input.mp4')
overlay_file = ffmpeg.input('overlay.png')
(ffmpeg
    .concat(
        in_file.trim(start_frame=10, end_frame=20),
        in_file.trim(start_frame=30, end_frame=40),
    )
    .overlay(overlay_file.hflip())
    .drawbox(50, 50, 120, 120, color='red', thickness=5)
    .output('out.mp4')
    .run()
)
```
As you can see you can use the `overlay` filter.

You can read `overlay`'s documentation in [ffmpeg website](https://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg-filters.html#overlay-1)

To answer my original question, it's simply a matter of `unzip file.docx`, and then the images are all in `word/media`.

However, replace_pic doesn't seem to work - it could barely find any of the images in my docx.  So for anyone else trying to swap images in a docx, here's how I did it:

You need to use replace_media instead.  Unfortunately, replace_media also needs a path to the original image that you want to replace, so I found the easiest way to do this was just to extract all the images from the docx in the code, something like this:

```
      from docxtpl import DocxTemplate
      from zipfile import ZipFile
      import shutil

      input_doc = DocxTemplate(in_docx)
      with ZipFile(in_docx_file, "r") as zipt:
        zipt.extractall(path="unzipt")

      # imagemap is a dict of old image filename to new image filename, like {"image10.jpg": "new image.png"}
      for src_filename, dest_filename in imagemap.items():
        print("replace image {0} with {1}".format(src_filename, dest_filename))
        src_filename = "unzipt/word/media/" + src_filename

        input_doc.replace_media(src_filename, dest_filename)
      shutil.rmtree("unzipt")
```

- os.replace calls MoveFileExW (which is just the unicode version of
  MoveFileEx) with the exact same flags as this library does:
  https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Modules/posixmodule.c

- fatomic calls MoveFileEx with the exact same flags as this library does:
  https://github.com/abarnert/fatomic/blob/master/fatomic.py#L29

- Whether MoveFileEx is atomic is addressed in the README. TLDR: For lack of
  alternatives we use that.

Not exactly the same bug, but I also encountered problems with debug printing of packets with unicode characters in the ```_log``` method of ```_AbstractTransport``` in version 0.5.6. The exception is thrown because the log message is of type ```unicode``` (since the url is stored in unicode), while the arguments for the log message are instead of type ```str```. This throws a unicode decode error. As workaround I use the following monkey patch:
```
from socketIO_client import transports
def _log_utf8(self, level, msg, *attrs):
    transports._log.log(level, '[%s] %s' % (self._url, msg), *[a.decode('utf-8') for a in attrs])
transports._AbstractTransport._log = _log_utf8
```

That's true. But please remember that under the hood PhonenumberField is actually CharField child class and value is stored in database according to `PHONENUMBER_DB_FORMAT` setting. By default it is E164 a.k.a. international format. The `contains` and `startswith` lookups are default CharField lookups so they look according to theirs default behaviour and looks through data in format defined by your settings.

A side note here: I really thing that it is a bad idea to change it to any other format because you might loose some part of your data. Assume two countries has the same number formats or same area code. Replacing international format with local and setting `PHONENUMBER_DEFAULT_REGION` may cause that number from e.g. Canada will be presented as US one. Loosing data in IT is really, really bad 😉. I've been thinking lately about changing a lot of PhonenumbersField internals... but till now I couldn't find enough time. Keep fingers crossed!

Looks good and I like that one can use the zdesk client plus you have patched the call method to work through pagination. On that, please note that the way Zendesk's APIs multi-page end-points return responses is different for their Core API and Incremental Export API. For Core, they simply set the next_page to null so that you don't make additional calls (and this is well handled in the zdesk client), for incremental export api, the next_page in the last page of the result set is set to a url which you are supposed to save and start getting the results from that point onwards (next time you run your fetch). You can also use the 'count' value to tally up the number of objects you have gathered so far :)

There's really only one narrow case where this PR keeps workers alive: if all runnable tasks are currently being run by other workers. If all of the tasks finish (success or fail) then the worker will die. This will only make a difference if you rely on retrying often and expect new tasks to switch from FAILED to PENDING while the tasks are running.

It seems that the identical effect could be achieved by simply adding a configuration parameter to count RUNNING tasks in the PENDING count in order to keep workers alive. Alternatively, we could add a timeout where workers stay alive until a configurable amount of time has passed without seeing any running tasks.

it depends how you think of the transform. If the matrix multiplications come after the point (i.e `point * transform` with `point` being a "row" vector) then it makes sense to say it converts from "mesh space to bone space", as it takes a point in mesh space and outputs a point in bone space. But when using matrix premultiplication as assimp does (`tranform * point` with `point` being a "column" vector) it makes more sense to think of it as transforming the space itself, from "bone space to mesh space", whereupon the point in mesh space can be used.

This is why the original poster is confused, because they are thinking about it backwards compared to how assimp treats point transformation. This is probably leading directly to their confusion with working out the transformation matrix of the bone and thinking this should be the same as mOffsetMatrix, when in fact it is the inverse.

This is all especially confusing as DirectX uses postmultiplication, and OpenGL uses premultiplication.

This all said, i hope that someone can update the documentation so that it makes it clear for everybody. It's very difficult to specify clearly without an "official" way of thinking about this sort of thing. I can't even find it documented anywhere how assimp treats transformation matrix multiplication... although the matrix notation used on the "Data Structures" page of the assimp docs impies premultipliction with column vectors.

- When `Page Should Contain Element` keyword `count is None` it should match to one or more elements. Just like the current keyword works

Hi @Akhilvis. You can get the latest available offset for a given partition with
```python
consumer.partitions[0].latest_available_offset() 
```
and you can check the offset of the last message consumed for a partition by accessing a private attribute:
```python
consumer._partitions[consumer.partitions[0]].last_offset_consumed
```
An implementation of `is_completed` based on these calls might look like this
```python
def is_completed(consumer):
    for partition in consumer.partitions:
        if consumer._partitions[partition].last_offset_consumed < partition.latest_available_offset():
            return False
    return True
```
We don't expose `_partitions` in the public API because the particular partition that a given message came from is a detail that Kafka clients prefer to obfuscate. If you're concerned about having consumed all messages from a given partition, your use case probably lends itself to using a single partition, at which point you'd be better off simply comparing `consumer.consume().offset` to `topic_obj.latest_available_offsets()`.

An update here (cc @magcius) - we've been discussing this change and while we all agree that it doesn't cause any new problems, it also doesn't truly solve the issue you're experiencing. This is mainly because of the possibility of out-of-order message production, which would make a naive comparison between the returned `Message` and the one from the delivery report fail. Additionally, returning a non-`None` value from a side-effecting method such as `produce` is not the most intuitive API design choice, and we would still like to reserve the possibility of further fleshing out the `Message` implementation before we make it so readily available via a public API.

Currently the best way to solve the original issue on this ticket is to count the number of produced messages and compare it to the number of delivery reports every so often. We will keep the pull request open and hopefully merge it in the future when the `Message` interface is a bit more well-developed.

@cdeil @joleroi @keflavich - I've now made a small package called [pytest-fits](https://github.com/astrofrog/pytest-fits) (a spin-off of [pytest-mpl](https://github.com/astrofrog/pytest-mpl)) which can be used to easily deal with checking that the array or HDU output of functions is correct, and makes it easy to generate reference files. I developed it for the reproject package but I think it can be useful here too. Basically, with this plugin, you can write a test like:

```python
@pytest.mark.fits_compare
def test_succeeds():
    region = ...
    return region.to_mask(...)
```

then you can run the tests the first time with:

```
py.test --fits --fits-generate-path=tmp
```

and the tmp directory will contain the reference outputs. You can check these visually, and if they are ok, then they can be moved to a ``baseline`` sub-directory in the tests folder. Then, just run the tests with:

```
py.test --fits
```

to make sure the output is compared to the reference. Note that you can also add ``--fits`` to ``setup.cfg`` so that this option gets included automatically - see [here](https://github.com/astrofrog/reproject/blob/master/setup.cfg#L14) for how the reproject package does it.

I note that region filters that pyregion generates are based on their image coordinate. And things like radius is converted to a radius in the image plane simply using the plate scale. So, you should not use pyregion's filter if the sky curvature is not negligible.

For pyregion, I guess the question is what should be its behavior when some of the input points are not defined in the image coordinate. My inclination is that we just raise an exception indicating that the filter is ill-defined. Thought?

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_lfw_people
from skimage.transform import resize

dataset = fetch_lfw_people(resize=1.0,
                           slice_=(slice(0, 250),
                                   slice(0, 250)))
images = dataset.images
indexes = np.random.randint(0, images.shape[0], size=100)

faces = []
non_faces = []
selected = []
for im_idx in indexes:
    im = images[im_idx] / 255
    faces.append(resize(
        im[70:195, 70:195],
        output_shape=(25, 25),
        anti_aliasing=False))
    non_faces.append(resize(
        im[0:50, 0:50],
        output_shape=(25, 25),
        anti_aliasing=False))
    selected.append(im)

faces = np.array(faces)
non_faces = np.array(non_faces)

np.save('cbcl_database.npy', np.concatenate((faces, non_faces)), axis=0)

This is something that rarely happens when upgrading from specific older versions. At one point we had a couple Python function names which now map to a Cython function of the same name or vice versa, or where a Cython `*.pyx` or `*.pyd` file was moved. Upgrading drops new, correct files in all the proper places, but old compiled files may hang around. Sometimes they get searched first, and then the import can stumble in an odd way.

If you experience this, please try the solution posted above by @mhejrati summarized by:

``` bash
rm -rf path/to/python/site-packages/skimage
pip install scikit-image
```

which nukes your entire skimage directory so you can start fresh. As there is a known solution, I'm closing the issue.

I want to reiterate though: this was always a rare problem and our current naming conventions make it unlikely to happen again. I have only experienced it once while developing between major versions.

On 2015-01-19 13:16:31, OOM <omer.ozak@gmail.com> wrote:
> I am trying IPython parallel since I think it should allow me to overcome
> the lack of memory in each node. E.g. If I define a parallel function like
>
> @dview.parallel(block=True)
> def costsnx(costs):
>     mcp=graph.MCP_Geometric(costs)
>     return mcp

I don't think this approach will work.  The MCP implementation is in
Cython, and IPython would not know how to split it up.  Also, it is not
trivially parallelizeable, so you can't split the input and have it work
out of the box.

That said, a straight-forward dynamic programming approach, such as the
one in ``graph.shortest_path`` uses approximately the same amount of
memory as the input, so may work on a single machine in your case.

It is possible to parallelize dynamic programming problems, but
typically you need lightweight threads--the parallelization gives you an
advantage with multiple cores, but is not that useful if you want to
spread memory use across multiple machines where the cost of launching
tasks is high.

For that approach, you may find some of these references interesting:

http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/summary?doi=10.1.1.301.6093
http://research.microsoft.com/apps/pubs/default.aspx?id=208241
http://www.shodor.org/petascale/materials/UPModules/dynamicProgrammingPartI/

Do you have any guidance on the appropriate API here?

This breaks at least two aspects of the API for `downscale_local_mean`:
- `factors` don't need to be integers.
- `cval` doesn't make sense.

Also, we can just as easily do upscaling with the local mean value as well downscaling, e.g., resizing `[0, 1]` to size 5 would result in `[0, 0, 0.5, 1, 1]`.

My initial thought was to make `cval=None` an indicator to do resizing with fractional overlap. But then there's the separate issue of non-integer factors. An API that allows non-integer factors only if cval is None seems very awkward. I don't like the value dependent semantics. I suppose we could probably support `cval` when padding by a fractional factors, but the implementation is not obvious to me and that's not a feature I'm excited to write.

My tentative proposal would be a new function, perhaps `resize_local_mean(image, output_shape)`.

  def _initializeBucketMap(self, maxBuckets, offset):

Hi Alexis.
I will do tests and documentation for this pull request. This pull request in my opinion allows you to group relevant methods for one resource that otherwise shouldn't exist out of scope for the defined resource. Think about sub-resources that are possible within RESTful scope. I.e. if you have depended resources that cannot otherwise exist without the main resource, you can define additional paths that serves this exact purpose. 

Let's suppose you have "Users" resource. The "User" has it's depended resources (sub-resources) like "Friends". If you make general methods for querying this user from the data source (db or otherwise) and the subresource "Friends" depends on selecting proper "User" by means of "id" field passed in matchdict, this could ease the development of methods for such resource. If you have a path="/users/{id}" and friends_path="/users/{id}/friends" you can then create method for selecting User object by it's "id" field and then query "friends" field or relation on the User.id == request.matchdict['id'] instance. 

I am aware that you can do this with Service classes, but it seems to me a little bit counterintuitive and leaves you with reimplementing select method for User object in each Service instance class that is relevant to all it's sub-resources.

If it it's ``False`` sessions expire with the browser session (i.e. non-persistent cookie), if you set it to a ``timedelta`` the the session is permanent (i.e.  persistent browser cookie). 

Flask is setting this [by default to 31 days](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/master/flask/app.py#L295), so most likely you are changing it explicitly in expire?

I think you have misunderstood what I tried to say.
Email support used to be directly built into the djoser package, but it wasn't a good design, because it wasn't part of this project's scope. Djoser's job ultimate goal is to provide a reference implementation of common REST endpoints relating to user authentication and that's the very reason why I've decided to delegate the task of email support to separate package, which is the [django-templated-mail](https://github.com/sunscrapers/django-templated-mail/) I've mentioned in my previous post.

Now, without email support in Djoser it didn't make sense to keep settings like `DOMAIN` or `SITE_NAME` in package settings, because the only place, where they have been used was code responsible for emails, so I've moved these settings to the [django-templated-mail](https://github.com/sunscrapers/django-templated-mail/) package.

So all you have to do is to define these settings in global scope instead of Djoser scope, e.g.:
```python
DOMAIN = 'frontend.com'
SITE_NAME = 'Frontend'

DJOSER = {
    'PASSWORD_RESET_CONFIRM_URL': '#/password/reset/confirm/{uid}/{token}',
    'ACTIVATION_URL': '#/activate/{uid}/{token}',
    'SEND_ACTIVATION_EMAIL': True,
}
```

Hopefully this clarifies the situation.

the way to setup a package is with

setup package_name -t eups_tag

setup GalSim -t lsst.1.2

I do want to clarify the reason for it that I had in mind. If only icons / shims / categories are all available as json files, they can be used to easily import it in any database structure. JSON is a supported syntax in many different database applications. YML is sad fully not, they need to be parsed out with external tools or converted before using them.

When importing the JSON files you have a full list of all the available icons, including all the categories. A database can then be build to display a list all available icons just like it's done on the following page: https://fontawesome.com/icons?d=gallery

You maybe think, why in earth you want to build a tool that maps the search capabilities like it is on the website and not use it in the website itself? Well the reason is that the SVG files that you download from the website are for some database application not compatible. The reason is that they are missing a width and height parameter in the svg tag. With the build database tool this can be for seen and a user can add it directly as SVG to the database application. 

Another note: our company has bought a pro license. The pro icons can not be downloading directly as SVG from the website. You need to have access to the project itself and browse in the correct directory in order to find the specified icon as SVG file. Off course the pro project can only be accessed by a limited amount of people. With the build database file (for internal use) it can be used by all employees in an easy manner. Even those with no knowledge of github or whatsoever. When all three json files that I listed above exist a script could be made that updates the database without manually interfering.

And one more thing:
```yaml
watchdog:
  safety_margin: -1
```

You shouldn't set `safety_margin` to a negative value.

Lets assume that something weird happened with Patroni on the master node and postgres now runs without Patroni supervision.

The purpose of watchdog is to restart such node before the leader key will expire.
If you have  safety_margin = -1, watchdog will restart the node only one second after expiration of leader key. During this second you will have to nodes running as a master...

Having said that, I don't want to discourage anyone from trying to bring new fancy features to parameters 🙂. 

If someone wants to do so, I'm not sure using the `parameters(period)...threshold_retirement[eta]` notation would be the best thing to do, as 
- `[]`, in many languages (including Python and JS) means accessing an object's property, and I'm not sure it would be straightforward to put complex logics behind it.
- The code between the `[]` notation is some quite complex numpy business, so adapting it to do what you want to do will be tough, at least if you want to enable things like  `parameters(period)...threshold_retirement[eta].some_subparam`. 

But there is another approach that allows experimentation 🙂. You can define a re-usable template function.

Let's keep the parameter file as Lorenzo suggested in the first post:

```yaml
<=70:
description: Threshold within the retirement income is not taxable for people whom has an age major lower than 70  
  values:
    2017-01-01: 8000.00
70<:
  description: Threshold within the retirement income is not taxable for people whom has an age major than 70
  values:
    2017-01-01: 7500.00
```

In a `templates.py` file, you can define templates

```py
def Steps(parameter):
    # Parse the parameter
    def function(array):
        # Apply it to any array
return function
```

And it your variable file:

```
class threshold_retirement_income_not_taxable(Variable):
   ...

    def formula(person,period,parameters):
        age= person('age',period)
        threshold= parameters(period).taxes.IRPEF.income_not_taxable. threshold_retirement 
        return Steps(threshold)(age)  #
```

This approach allows you to develop nice fancy features in your own codebase, avoiding high coupling with core. And such templates could be shared with others, and maybe distributed with core if the community seems it's a good idea. 

Honnestly, if I were to build the marginal scales today, I'd probably use this approach rather than putting them deep in core as they are right now, which often causes trouble 🙂

Other than the fact that `dataset.save(stuff)` feels more natural to me than `connection.save(stuff, dataset_id)` (which I have no rational argument for):

Eliminating dataset prevents us from allowing users to associate persistent settings on a per dataset basis, for example, eventually consistent reads, or forced mutations. Instead they have to specify these options each time they call the method in connection. 

In my mind a dataset was an immutable wrapper for all the optional arguments to mutations and fetches. So the interface for `save`, `delete`, etc can be much cleaner, while the API still has full functionality. In my api mock up, dataset needed to do a lot of work, not just keeping track of an id:

*keep track of read_options
*automatically attach partition_ids to keys (to support namespacing)
*keep track of mutation options
*super-class for transaction (which behaving similarly, in addition to keeping track of a transaction_id and sanity checking read/write options)
*factory for transactions and bulk mutators (clones over option fields to the new objects)

None of this functionality should be merged into connection, as it all depends on options which could reasonably be different depending on the dataset. 

All of this could be done with optional arguments to `connection` methods but it feels much cleaner to have them wrapped in an object.

basically trying to freeze the dependencies for the libraries we use for datascience (numpy and pandas) in a PEX locally, and then ideally use that PEX to build every binary that depends on them

should i just save those dependencies locally and point at them to replace the first step?

see the specs here:
  - https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0491/#file-name-convention
  - https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0425/#overview

the first field is the 'abi type' - `none` in the protobuf example and `cp35m` in the multidict. `none` indicates that it need not conform to an abi type because it's pure python code. `cp35m` indicates compatibility with CPython 3.5.x with pymalloc (m) and short (ucs2) unicode support.

the second field is the 'platform type' - `any` in the protobuf example indicates a "universal" wheel - or one that is pure python and can be executed by any python interpreter. `linux_x86_64` is the normalized name for `linux-x86_64`, which indicates this wheel carries platform-specific code (e.g. C extensions) that have been compiled specifically for linux-x86_64.

docx files are kind of fluid files, which means they do not determine, where each paragraph gets rendered. It's only possible to know the position of a text block, after the document gets rendered, e.g. in Word.
We are dealing with a similar problem. In the end, we are converting the docx to a pdf, a fixed layout format, and extract the location information from there. We use LibreOffice (via unoconv) for this, which works quite well if you have all needed fonts in place.

I don't think a sprite sheet would be compatible with using CSS selectors on the SVG: https://github.com/google/material-design-icons/tree/master/sprites#css-selectors-transformation-and-the-shadow-dom

Note that the 'svg inline' loader means that all icons are loaded at one time, so (like a sprite sheet) it prevents many small HTTP requests.

see also: https://css-tricks.com/inline-svg-best-svg/

data = open('TEXT_OUTPUT_MOSHE_1.txt', 'rb').read()
s = set(data)
b = set([chr(i) for i in range(256)])
x = []
for i in range(256):
  try:
    x.append(chr(i).encode('cp1252'))
  except:
    pass
x = set(x)
print ((b-s) & x)
```

You can use the `Beamformer` object on recorded signal as well.
```
'''
This example shows how to create delay and sum beamformers
'''
from __future__ import print_function, division

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.io import wavfile
import pyroomacoustics as pra

# Open your recorded signals
fs, my_recording = wavfile.read('my_recording.wav')

# Create a room around your array
# this is merely a convenience, it doesn't have to be the real room
room = pra.ShoeBox([4,6], fs=fs)

# Add a source somewhere in the room
# You don't need to know the exact location, just set
room.add_source(<coordinate of the source relative to your microphone array>)

# Create a linear array beamformer with 4 microphones
# with angle 0 degrees and inter mic distance 10 cm
R = <coordinates of the microphones on your array>
room.add_microphone_array(pra.Beamformer(R, room.fs))

# Add the recorded signals in your array
room.mic_array.signals = my_recording

# Now compute the delay and sum weights for the beamformer
# Use the far field option if you don't know the exact distance
# from source to microphone array
room.mic_array.rake_delay_and_sum_weights(room.sources[0][:1], far_field=True)

# alternatively you can compute your own weights and set them directly
# room.mic_array.weights = my_weights

# Now compute the output of the beamformer
output = room.mic_array.process()
```

@brawer I feel that you're overthinking and it's probably my fault. Let me restate the situation:
• I have a UFO font that is ready to be generated into an OTF.
• The [`features.fea` file](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/features.fea/) inside that UFO contains GSUB as well as GPOS features, namely `kern`, `mark` and `mkmk`.
• When I run this UFO thru **fontmake** using no options (`fontmake -u font.ufo`) it generates its own `kern`, `mark` and `mkmk` features and appends them to the already existing FEA code, resulting in two sets of GPOS features.
• To prevent **fontmake** from appending its generated GPOS features, I use the classes described in the original comment by saving them in a file, and running this command instead, `fontmake -u font.ufo --kern-writer-module MyGPOSModule --mark-writer-module MyGPOSModule`, which essentially results in the appending of an empty string, therefore leaving the original code in `features.fea` intact.

That said, I'm looking for a way of telling **fontmake**, *Thanks, but I don't want the GPOS feature code that you generate at build time*. The solution I have in place works fine, but I feel that there must be a simpler way of implementing this, likely as an option, which could benefit other users of **fontmake**.
Note that variable fonts are generated (have checked how good they are).

And now you can see why, if we implement markers as just an extension of MatchSpec, we need to inject the additional platform and system information into the solver index.

Interesting. For more context, this is a problem with [_conda-forge's_ `vc` metapackages](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/vc/files), which use something akin to the [variant metapackage approach](https://gist.github.com/mcg1969/20667002c79f702e3c7003c81b5f24c8) as well. As I indicate there, the solution is to somehow prevent variants from being scheduled for upgrades in a `conda update --all` situation.

This seems like a good way to address the problem, but it is not scalable. We can't support adding new lines to `conda` code every time someone decides to create a new variant metapackage. We need a way, probably in conda metadata, to _identify_ these variant metapackages and handle them using this logic automatically.

I recommend using a shell-scripted loop, then using `curator_cli`, rather than the full version of Curator that uses a YAML-based action file.

The approach would be to build a list of indices with one iteration that uses a `--filter_list` to select indices, just as you're doing now.  Then on each iteration of the loop, call 
```
curator_cli allocation [OPTIONS] --filter_list \{\"filtertype\":\"pattern\",\"kind\":\"regex\",\"value\":\"\^$INDEXNAME\$\"\}
```
where `$INDEXNAME` is the index from the loop.

It might look something like this:

```
for INDEX in $(curator_cli show_indices --filter_list \{\"filtertype\":\"pattern\",\"kind\":\"regex\",\"value\":\"\^netflow\"\}); do
  curator_cli allocation [OPTIONS] --filter_list \{\"filtertype\":\"pattern\",\"kind\":\"regex\",\"value\":\"\^$INDEX\$\"\}
```

This loop will iterate over all indices that start with `netflow`.  You can add more filters by making them a JSON array—effectively translating what you have in the YAML to a single string.

Obviously, you'd have to replace `[OPTIONS]` with what you want for your allocation, but this will do a single action at a time, like you're asking.

The issue is that while `/_cluster/state` is exposed in Amazon ES 5 the`/_cluster/state/metadata` call returns everything but Settings for indices.  This fails because Curator tries to pull the index settings from the metadata [here](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/87e652711cd580cec9fadedaaf2d38f5f163e169/curator/indexlist.py#L149), which is why you're getting "KeyError: 'settings'" error. 

AWS is aware of the issue so hopefully it'll be supported soon.

Without the coding magic comment defined by [PEP263](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0263/), encoding of Python files is considered ASCII for Python 2 and UTF8 for Python 3 (see [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#source-file-encoding) for reference).

So there is two ways to fix this issue and respect Python spec:
- Add an "UTF8" magic comment
- Fix file to be pure ASCII (since this is the same files for Py2 and Py3, ASCII is required)

I agree with @devigned that the most simple is to fix the files so they are pure ASCII.

@devigned makes me think that I need to check what Autorest is doing if there is an UTF8 character in the Swagger (that's not forbidden). Guess in this case the best fix is to add the magic comment in Python in all generated files, to keep the description as close as possible of the Swagger file. Otherwise every UTF8 characters will be replaced by a '?'

The other thing to keep in mind is that the behaviors we support are unlike
any other selection/cursor UI:

* In text editors, the cursor is only the insertion point - you have to
perform a selection before you can perform any actions (cut/copy/paste).
Our focused cell acts both as a cursor *and* all actions apply to it by
default. Also, in a text editor, the cursor is something that is always
between units (letters). Our cursor is much more like that of Gmail's in
that it is *on* not between the elements being selected.
* In selection UIs such as Gmail, the cursor also does not apply actions.

IOW, our behavior for this is unlike anything else.

> Lots of thoughts...
>
> The most important cell is the one that has focus. Traditionally we have
> called this cell the "selected" cell. It has two purposes or roles:
>
> A) It maintains focus and is the cell that will be edited if the user
> presses "Enter".
> B) By default cell level actions apply to it.
>
> Previously, we used a single visual indicator for both of these roles
> (grey border).
>
> In the new multiple-selection behavior we have agreed upon, these two
> roles are being separated.
>
> * The focused cell has both of these roles.
> * Contiguous blocks of cells (that include the focused cell) can also have
> the second role.
>
> Important things should be indicated as such visually. Less important
> things should be indicated as such visually.
>
> In my mind, even with the new behaviors, the focused cell is the most
> important one. Actions always apply to to, it is the cell that will be
> edited, the page will scroll as the user moves it and it is the cursor/head
> for multiple selections. Because of this, it should have the most prominent
> visual design.
>
> The other cells to which actions will apply should have a less prominent
> visual design than the focused cell as they do fewer things.
>
> My problem with the current design is that the visual design of the action
> cells is the equivalent of yelling. The extremely strong visual treatment
> of these cells is inconsistent with the more subtle design choices we make
> everywhere else *and* it is confusingly more prominent than that focused
> cell.
>

A couple of things on the transactions. The primary motivation for transactions is that they allow the server component to handle batches of commands. The KMIP spec defines many operations that can be performed, and it allows a client to perform multiple operations in a single request. This can be useful for doing an operation like _create_ a key and then add an _attribute_ to it.

The transaction is primarily needed so that if there are multiple steps then we can rollback any changes if any of the steps fail. I feel like this is high priority for the system.

If we want to support concurrency in the future then this also provides that by means of the database transactions.

> How practical is supporting transactions on a non-relational backend?

How many other backends do we really anticipate? I do not think creating a memory backend is worthwhile. You could do it, but why not use the DB repo with an in-memory database?

We could have a non-relational database, but why would anyone do that except for fun? If they did choose something else then I would hope that the database architecture supports transactions, and I'm sure that it would.

In summary I think if another backend is developed then I imagine it will not be a memory backend, and it will be some sort of non-relational database that has transaction support. I think the transaction support is crucial for batches of commands.

Hello,

unfortunately you always need the event_loop fixture, even if you don't ask for it explicitely (because you need it to run your test and fixtures). 

From the docs (a little buried I admit): All scopes are supported, but if you use a non-function scope you will need to redefine the event_loop fixture to have the same or broader scope. Async fixtures need the event loop, and so must have the same or narrower scope than the event_loop fixture.

Try redefining the event_loop fixture like this:

```
@pytest.yield_fixture(scope='class')
def event_loop(request):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy().new_event_loop()
    yield loop
    loop.close()
```

What if there was a pytest fixture or context manager? Then we could have

```py
@pytest.mark.parametrize('seed', [9001, 1337])
def test_old_bad_seeds(seed, randomly):
    randomly.reseed(seed)
    # test that has failed in the past with those exact seeds
```

Tbh I'm not that excited about this though. I think if you find a failing test with a particular *seed*, it's not the *seed* that should be turned into a new test case but the actual underlying data that caused the failure. For example if seed 9001 caused your data generator to create a user with a really long name, add a test with a user with a fixed really long name.

Fixing the seed in a test run is pretty fragile, as the data generator could change in the future, meaning the seed values don't mean the same thing, so the tests could stop testing the specific bugs they exposed. For example, new versions of `faker` come with new data, remapping the data returned for a given seed.

Do you have a use case that prevents making tests with fixed test data, or at makes it impractical?

@Telofy It's not actually that the default year is overriding the parsed year, it's that the default year, when unspecified, is the current year, and the date you were parsing happened to be the current year when you were parsing it.

The problem is that `2016:` is not being picked up, which it definitely should be, especially when `fuzzy` is included. I'm getting some weird results, though:

```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>>  parse('18 October 1874:', fuzzy_with_tokens=True)
(datetime.datetime(2016, 10, 18, 0, 0), (' ',))
```

What's very strange is that when it's fuzzy, it ignores the `YYYY:` section entirely, and doesn't even consider it a token. I can understand ignoring it and considering it a token, or (even better) not ignoring it, but ignoring it and *not* having it be a token is a bit puzzling.

Gets even weirder, though, because when you add additional tokens that should be ignored, it starts throwing a `ValueError`, but only when the colon is present there:

```python
>>> parse('18 October 1874: Had to eat dogs', fuzzy_with_tokens=True)
...
ValueError: Unknown string format
>>> parse('18 October 1874 Had to eat dogs', fuzzy_with_tokens=True)
(datetime.datetime(1874, 10, 18, 0, 0), (' ', 'Had to eat dogs'))
```

Definitely bizarre. As a stop-gap measure, if you haven't already fixed your issue, maybe try stripping `:` off of the end of any strings you are parsing - they will never be meaningful anyway, but this bug definitely needs to be fixed.

@alanfranz Returning just whats there at the time of the timeout instead of raising an exception is explicit design decision and not an accident. It is inspired by Python way of handling EOF on files or sockets `recv` method that returns what's there and maybe less than you wanted. You can tell if a timeout occurred or not by checking if you got what you wanted, e.g. the number of bytes requested for `read`. If it's less, it's a timeout.

This issue is essentially the question "how do we interpret shapes". In this permute case, Keras wants to permute a shape from `(10,64,3)` to `(3,64,10)`; but because Keras uses `(height, width, channels)` for image feature axis while CoreML uses `(channels, height, width)`, there are two ways to interpret what `Permute` in Keras is really doing:
(1) It wants the output tensor to be an image feature map with `height=3, width=64, channels=10`. In this case, the converter should respect the meaning of the axes, so the output shape should be `(10,3,64)`. 
(2) It wants the output tensor to be exactly of shape `(3,64,10)` with no meaning on the axes. In this case, the converter should respect the data layout in memory, so the output shape should be `(3,64,10)`, same as Keras. 
At this point the converter chooses to use (1) where the meaning of axes are respected, simply because if a Keras model has convolutions following `Permute` or `Reshape`, the converted CoreML model has the correct `(C,H,W)` axes to do the convolution.

### Optionals vs. Alternatives

Alternatives:

* Alternatives are surrounded by parenthesis, like `what is your (home|cell) phone number`
* At least one of the parts of the alternative must be in the user's message for matching to occur
* The captured value from the message goes into a `<star>` tag.

Optionals:

* Optionals are surrounded by square brackets, like `what is your [home|cell] phone number`
* At least one part of the optional, *or none of them at all*, must be in the user's message for matching to occur.
  * Example: `what is your home phone number` matches
  * Example: `what is your phone number` matches
  * Example: `what is your office phone number` does not match
* The captured value of the optional *does not* go into a `<star>` tag.

Under the hood these features are very similar: in the alternative form, the regular expression looks exactly the same as the source, like:

```perl
/^what is your (home|cell) phone number$/
```

For optionals, the regular expression adds a bunch of extra metacharacters to make sure that it doesn't get captured into a `<star>` tag (by surrounding it with `(?:...)`, where the `?:` makes it non-capturing), and additional characters are inserted to detect word boundaries and whitespace to make it possible that the user's message *does not* contain any of the parts of the optional at all (by putting the sequence `(?:\s|\b)+` on either side of each part, as well as including a new part that consists *only* of `(?:\s|\b)` to catch the word not being in the message at all).

```perl
/^what is your(?:(?:\s|\b)+home(?:\s|\b)+|(?:\s|\b)+cell(?:\s|\b)+|(?:\s|\b))phone number$/
```

The documentation should probably be updated to make it clearer that optionals can have alternatives inside them.

### Sort Order for Wildcards

> Assuming descending order of priority, do you agree on the following order?
>
> `atomic > _ > _ _ > _ * > _ _ * > *`

Generally when you get down to just sorting the wildcard-only triggers, the order of priority for the types of wildcards goes `_ > # > *`, with the `*` at the end because it would also match the same things `_` and `#` match.

The order of triggers you listed would match the following types of messages in this order:

1. Atomic triggers
2. The user said a single word
3. The user said two words (separated by a space)
4. The user said one word, and then one or more of anything
5. The user said two words, and then one or more of anything
6. Anything else the user said that wasn't matched.

Step 5 above would not be matched because step 4 would've also matched any messages that match on step 5. So I'd probably switch those around; but if this is difficult I wouldn't worry too much about it, as the tail end of the sort list has always been a sort of grey edge-case area anyway.

Granted. I had in mind an intent that doesn't match the implementation. So from what I understand you are right. 

The intent I had in mind was that Z3Objects maintain a pointer to a structure that owns a reference count and an unmanaged pointer to the context. The Context should do that too. Then the unmanaged context goes out of scope when the last reference to this structure goes out of scope.

As it is now, the context is an attribute on Z3Object so it never goes out of scope until the last reference to it is gone, and the Context class is not tracking its own reference counts. The scenarios you describe say what is problematic about this. I am somewhat puzzled why this doesn't get exposed.

Given that AST and other objects inherit from Z3Object and rely on other attributes of Context I am not sure if a simple change suffices (maybe "close" is the answer, but I don't have experience with this) or we should examine the reference counting discipline on the Context object, which currently does not own a reference count, or consider intermediary objects that own the unmanaged context pointer and reference counting queues (because inc-ref and dec-ref on Z3 pointers are not thread safe and GC happens in separate threads).

Another note: the use of reference counts is different in Context.cs and Context.java. It is used in the dispose method in Context.java and the finalize method in Context.cs.

`using_transactions` is really just the answer to the question "Are we currently inside an `@atomic` block?", which is only known inside `import_data()`, so it can't be assigned only in the resource constructor.

Since the semantics of `using_transactions` is only relevant within the scope of an import call, there's no reason to make it a property of the `Resource` class. The natural state of a `Resource` is orthogonal to how it gets imported.

We pass `using_transactions` as a parameter next to wherever (the now correct value for) `dry_run` is being passed, so existing clients can adjust behaviour appropriately.

In the Dirac script it checks for successful submission. If it is not successful then the error message gets returned in the dict for the subjob. Subsequently when the Dirac IDs are being applied to the jobs, if the dict for the subjob contains a string then the submission for that subjob is marked as failed and the error message printed. I have a branch in which I list Ansible 2.0.0-0.7.rc2 as a dependency and use Ansible's Python API to implement an `execute_command` function. See: https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/compare/master...Ichimonji10:ansible Everything works, as is noted by the commit message in that branch:

> Add in a hacky bit of logic to execute commands on a remote system. Sample usage:
>
> ```python
> >>> from pulp_smash.config import ServerConfig
> >>> cfg = ServerConfig(base_url='localhost')
> >>> from pulp_smash.utils import execute_command
> >>> execute_command('echo foo', cfg)
> 0
> >>> execute_command('ls /etc', cfg)
> 0
> >>> execute_command('ls /foo', cfg)
> 2
> ```

That said, I'm going to walk back from using Ansible for now, and see if simply using system shells and SSH via the subprocess module will suffice. To understand why, it's worth reviewing the benefits I had in mind when exploring Ansible, and the costs associated with using Ansible.

The benefits are as follows:

* Ansible provides a mechanism for transparently managing systems via both SSH and a local shell. (And others too.)
* Ansible is easy to use. Its basic usage via the CLI is dead simple, and it has a moderate learning curve. It is likely that its API is similarly simple, given that the CLI is implemented through the API.
* Pulp already contains Ansible playbooks and roles. If Pulp Smash makes use of Ansible too, we might be able to re-use some of that code.
* Ansible offers some abstractions, such as the ability to manage services regardless of whether the backing init system is System V, upstart or systemd.

Of these four benefits, only one has panned out. It's true that Ansible provides a mechanism for transparently managing systems via both SSH and a local shell. All you need to do is create an inventory file listing the hosts to manage and add some settings into the inventory file. The other three haven't panned out:

* Ansible's CLI and API have drastically different levels of complexity. The API documentation is flimsy, there are no discussions of the v2 API on the mailing lists, and the code itself is basically undocumented (sometimes copied-and-pasted; other times just wrong). I've found myself walking through Ansible's CLI implementation in order to reverse-engineer the API.
* Pulp does already contain Ansible playbooks and roles, but I don't think we'll need to use them. We need to do things like start, stop and inspect services, and inspect files on the filesystem. Ansible playbooks are used to chain together actions on hosts, and roles are used to group hosts and assign variables in interesting ways. We don't need that.
* Ansible does offer some nice abstractions in the form of modules. But the modules that are shipped with Ansible are oriented toward declaring state, not inspecting systems. You can declare that a service should be stopped or that a file should exist; you cannot ask what the current state of a service is or what the permissions on a file are. This is in line with Ansible's philosophy of crafting idempotent deployment-oriented code. You might be able to get state information anyway with facts, but should things really be this hard?

Excellent work. I have just one request, and it's a bit of a pain: rename `PulpSmashConfig.selinux_enabled` to `PulpSmashConfig.pulp_selinux_enabled`. This lets the new variable abide by the same naming scheme as `PulpSmashConfig.pulp_auth` and `pulp_version`.

To understand the naming scheme, consider how one gets at the data within an in-memory config file:

```
>>> import json
>>> with open('/home/ichimonji10/.config/pulp_smash/settings.json') as handle:
...     config_file = json.load(handle)
...
>>> config_file['pulp']['auth']
['admin', 'admin']
>>> config_file['pulp']['version']
'2.16.1'
>>> config_file['pulp']['selinux_enabled']
True
```

A `PulpSmashConfig` object represents the entire configuration file structure. Just as one would execute `config_file['pulp']['auth']`, one can execute `pulp_smash_config.pulp_auth`.

1) On conda-forge, we like packages that support it to provide `projectConfig.cmake` files so that other packages built with cmake can find them, and I am diligent about that.

    Examples:

    `libzmq:` https://github.com/conda-forge/libzmq-feedstock
    `cppzmq:` https://github.com/conda-forge/cppzmq-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml#L30
    `rapijson`: https://github.com/conda-forge/rapidjson-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml#L24
    `cryptopp`: https://github.com/conda-forge/cryptopp-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml#L38

and more (clang, xeus, llvm)...

This allows dependencies to find them at build time when their conda-recipe performs a cmake install.

2) Now, certain packages like `pybind11` are distributed on pypi and conda. The build of the conda recipe only runs setup.py which relies on distutils `install_headers` command, which results in headers being put at the distutils location in the conda environement. (Which is `$PREFIX/include` on unix and `%LIBRARY_INCLUDE%` on windows typically).

For people who make packages depending on pybind11 assuming the scheme used on pypi, it is important to *not* change that.

3) But now that pybind11 also has a nice cmake recipe, it is able to produce the crucial `pybind11Config.cmake` files. It would be nice if those were also in the conda package so that clients to pybind11 could use cmake's `find_package` to find pybind11...

Therefore, I opened this PR which enables (now behind an opt-in flag) the cmake installation at the same location as with setup.py while producing cmake project config files to find them.

Since these cmake project files go in a cmake configuration directory under the conda environment prefix, they are local to the conda environment where things are installed and cannot conflict with another python or cmake installation.
