Minor thing: do note that `keepends=True` was intentional and `"\n".join` is not equivalent:

```python
from difflib import ndiff

a = "one\ntwo\nthree\n"
b = "ore\ntree\nemu\n"

print(r"''.join + keepends=True:")
print("".join(ndiff(a.splitlines(keepends=True), b.splitlines(keepends=True))))
print(r"'\n'.join + keepends=False:")
print("\n".join(ndiff(a.splitlines(), b.splitlines())))
```
Output:
```
''.join + keepends=True:
- one
?  ^
+ ore
?  ^
- two
- three
?  -
+ tree
+ emu

'\n'.join + keepends=False:
+ ore
- one
- two
- three
?  -

+ tree
+ emu
```

The second adds extra new lines after `?` lines and some diffs may change. A Python 2.7 compatible fix would be [`splitlines(True)` without the arg name](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/difflib.html#difflib.ndiff). It's a minor thing so I'm not sure if it's worth a whole new PR, but I can submit one if you'd like.

> The templated way of potentially adding the extra member and enabling/disabling functions in the tuple caster seems somewhat overkill to me. How about something much more old school? If implicit.h is included, #define PYBIND11_IMPLICIT_CONVERSIONS 1 (set to 0 by default, e.g. in common.h).
> 
> Next, add a constant to the template parameters of the tuple caster: int ImplicitConversions = PYBIND11_IMPLICIT_CONVERSIONS. This ensures that the types are different (i.e. no ABI problem).

I've been thinking about solutions to this, and I think it's much trickier than that.  Whether we do the above or use the templated approach, we just back up the ODR problem one step but don't eliminate it--the function initializing the tuple caster would then have different definitions: in one translation unit the local `tuple_caster<std::tuple<...>>` variable is a `tuple_caster<std::tuple<...>, void, 0>` and in the other it's `tuple_caster<std::tuple<...>, void, 1>`.  I explored how far this goes--we'd need to add a similar template parameter to cpp_function::initialize(), which then requires template parameters to the cpp_function constructors, which then requires template parameter for everything in pybind constructing cpp_function (module::def, class_, keep_alive_impl), which then cascades into detail::process_attributes.

And even then, I'm not sure I've tracked everything down.  The point is, it becomes a maintenance nightmare and makes the code more fragile (new could would have to similarly worry about it when calling any of the above) that I'm not even going to try to suggest is worthwhile.  It does have the advantage of allowing you to link implicit-cpp-conversion-enabled code with -disabled code, and lets you enable it via a header include rather than a build time definition.  That could be nice in a large project where implicit conversion is useful in just one part.

So I'll keep plugging away at it to see if I can come up with another approach that minimizes the object size, but if not, I think this will have to work via a compile-time define.

Sure, makes sense. Simple submodules can be used just to avoid name clashes in future tests. I took a brief look at maybe splitting the existing tests into submodules, but it wouldn't really add any value at this point -- future tests can easily just add submodules as needed.
No one has commented, and this seems fairly harmless (and fixes a bug, if a somewhat obscure one), so I'll plan to merge this later today.

As for the other patch: PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER sets up a Type value, if Type is trivially destructible, we could stash the implicit converted value there with an equivalent of new (&value) OutputType(...).  If not trivially destructible, though, that's not safe--so either we'd have to have `Type *value` for everything, or a mix depending on whether Type is trivially destructible.

Either way, I don't think it's going to save a whole lot--I can't see eliminating one pointer per type_caster instantiation cutting down the .so size a lot.  I think the approach I just pushed is nicer: it's cleaner (general type_casters don't need any support code at all, everything is done by the tuple type_caster), and it only increases the object size for code that actually wants to use the feature.

Here's a `certbot-auto` testing brain dump. If I forget anything please add it to this issue or if anyone working on this stuff has any questions, let me know. Some of the pain points I describe below should be taken into account when redesigning these tests.

There are two kinds of `certbot-auto` tests. First are the tests in `letsencrypt-auto-source/tests/auto_test.py` and the other are test farm tests in `tests/letstest/scripts/`. Both of these methods test `certbot-auto` as a whole rather than testing individual pieces.

#### letsencrypt-auto-source/tests/auto_test.py

This modifies pieces of `certbot-auto` to make testing easier. It uses a dummy PyPI server, GitHub server, signatures, and has `certbot-auto` install a simple Python package that spits out a version number rather than the real version of Certbot and all of its Python dependencies. All OS dependencies are still installed (if expected by the test).

For each test, a new directory is used for `certbot-auto`'s installation, but any installed OS packages persist between tests. We have Dockerfiles to run these tests on different systems in `letsencrypt-auto-source`. Feel free to add new ones to the `master` branch and then configure it to be run in the `.travis.yml` in `test-everything` branch as appropriate.

#### tests/letstest/scripts/

All of our "test farm" tests are found here but the ones specifically for testing `certbot-auto` that we currently run are `test_leauto_upgrades.sh` and `test_letsencrypt_auto_certonly_standalone.sh`. Feel free to modify these (as long as you're not deleting existing valuable tests) or add new ones.

There are a number of pain points for writing test farm tests in general such as it takes minutes to get the results of the tests after making changes and sometimes the connection to the EC2 instances failing, but I won't go into more detail about that kind of stuff here. Instead, there are a few gotchas specifically for `certbot-auto` tests you should be aware of.

First, you probably want to include `--no-self-upgrade` on the command line to prevent `certbot-auto` from downgrading itself from the version in `master` to the latest released version. Second, if you've added a flag to `certbot-auto` that Certbot needs to quietly accept, you can't test this without modifying `certbot-auto` because the latest released version of Certbot is pinned in the script. Third, make sure you're testing `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto` as the versions of `*-auto` in the root of our repository only change during releases.

#### general gotchas

In addition to the test farm test specific pain points, there are a few additional general ones. We don't currently test a real `certbot-auto` upgrade where phase 1 of the latest released (or older) version downloads the new version of the script and reruns itself starting at phase 2. This could potentially be simulated by running an old `certbot-auto`, replacing it with the new one, and running the new script with expected CLI flags (including `--le-auto-phase2`) but this hasn't been done or tested.

Partially because we're testing the script as a whole, there's not a great way to test OS specific functionality. This isn't done at all currently in `letsencrypt-auto-source/tests/auto_test.py` and in the test farm tests it is done by using the `OS_TYPE` environment variable, but this only gives you a high level description of the system. See `tests/letstest/scripts/test_apache2` for an example of this.

Another minor gotcha is because the name of the script being tested is `letsencrypt-auto`, non-interactive mode is assumed for backwards compatibility reasons. You can change the name of the script to literally anything else to work around this.

This is long and a bit unorganized, but I hope it helps.

>  Tell me the problematic domain (certbot does when it reports errors, but not when it gets an error from boulder)

I agree. This can be fixed either by having `boulder` include the problematic domain or Certbot wrapping the returned error message. Certbot is able to wrap the error message to include the problematic domain in the [current version of ACME implemented by Let's Encrypt](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-01) as each domain is requested individually. I'm not sure this is possible on our side in later versions of ACME, however, as all domains are requested at once by submitting a CSR.

> Ideally, I should get a single message for a single problem, not two possible errors for the same problem (see below for an actual instance of this)

I think this problem would have to be resolved by https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder. Certbot is just reporting the error returned by Let's Encrypt and while we could make assumptions about what other problems might be, this is CA dependent so I think it's best solved on their end.

> It should be possible for certbot to report errors to users in a language other than English (this means that boulder should be reporting errors by identifier + message instead of just message)

This is a hard one. See #115 for the more general problem on the Certbot side. If you want `boulder` to change their error format, you should open an issue either on their repo and/or send a message to the [ACME mailing list](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/search/?email_list=acme) if what you have in mind involves a protocol change.

> Explain the problem in English (not RFC-speak)

Here's what Certbot is doing here. It is taking the error type returned by the ACME CA and using it to include information about what the problem is in the general sense. This is where "The request message was malformed" comes from for `malformed` errors.

The other part of the error message comes from the CA. This is included in the detail field of the error as described in [ACME](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-04#section-5.7) and the linked RFC. With this in mind, you're proposing we rely entirely on the `detail` field provided by the CA to explain the problem to the user?

> This whole process is spread across two to four repositories (certbot/certbot, certbot/website, letsencrypt/boulder, letsencrypt/website). There's no good place for me to manage this process.

You're right that this problem touches multiple repos, however, as I described in certbot/website#184, I think the most important thing here is to improve the error messages. In my opinion, if the error messages are good enough, you don't need to document the issue on the Certbot or Let's Encrypt websites. If you agree, this problem only affects the certbot/certbot and letsencrypt/boulder repos.

> Imo, letsencrypt/boulder is abusing malformed (which is suggested for a bad nonce) in ACME

I don't think this is correct as their a `badNonce` error type specifically for this problem, however, feel free to open an issue against the letsencrypt/boulder repo if you disagree.

Great questions. The recommended way now to display and clear output inside a widget is to use an Output widget. We should add a note in the changelog.

Here's a brief example:

```python
from ipywidgets import Output, IntSlider, VBox
from IPython.display import clear_output
out = Output()

slider = IntSlider()

def square(change):
    with out:
        clear_output()
        print(change.new*change.new)

slider.observe(square, 'value')
slider.value = 50
display(VBox([slider, out]))
```

Supporting serial in one way or another would be great, though unfortunately it's not something I'm super familiar with myself. My impression from PySerial is that the code to support this would be somewhat complicated and specialized, so it might be something that makes more sense to keep in a specialized library on top of trio, rather than adding an API directly to trio itself? Certainly it makes sense to make it as similar as possible to a using a socket, for users, and we should reuse the `trio.abc.Stream` API. But I believe the operating system APIs for serial support are generally their own special things, so we couldn't literally reuse the socket code.

Looking at [the code for `asyncserial`](https://github.com/m-labs/asyncserial/blob/master/asyncserial/asyncserial.py), it looks like on Unix it would be pretty straightforward to write a basic, trio-friendly serial `Stream` on top of PySerial. On Windows, it would be a bit more difficult, but also doable – the issue is that on Windows you need to use IOCP, and the trio core has support for this in principle but no one has actually used it yet so it probably needs some work.

Serial support isn't something that I anticipate having the time to work on soon myself, but if others want to then hopefully that gives some idea where to start, and of course please ask here or in chat if you need more information or get stuck anywhere.
